title: 2026/05/05 [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2026-05-05 - Justice Committee - Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill [Part 2]
description: Tuesday, 5 May 2026 | Justice Committee Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Kay Jones Elizabeth Walker Steve Farrow Craig Atkinson WeCreate Inc Paula Browning, Executive Director Greater Wellington Regional Council Daran Ponter, Chair Claire Johnstone, Councillor Fiona Robinson-Morey, Principal Advisor Local Government New Zealand Mayor Fran Wilde Mayor Grant Smith Te Ngira Institute for Population Research Polly Atatoa Carr, Associate Professor New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties Steven Ensslen, Deputy Chair Alan Keegan Tigger Wells Blind Citizens NZ Rose Wilkinson, Chief Executive Jonathan Godfrey, President Godfrey Bridger Research Association of New Zealand Ian Mills, Chair Penny Turner, RANZ Member and Director of the Navigators It's in the Ballot Productions Sam Somers, Co-Chair Anna Muir
descriptionSummary: Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill. Kay Jones. Elizabeth Walker

chunks.text: Very full on volume. Thank you. Thank you. Now, I'll just start with giving an example of why it's important to be counted. So I'm older, my parents are dead. I'm not going to be kicked out of my home if I disclose information about my identity. That's not the situation for everybody. I've come here today wearing a T-shirt saying, "Challenge biphobia."

Even wearing that in some countries in the world would be enough to get me put in jail. But that's not Aotearoa, New Zealand. However, it is important that we recognize why people, particularly those who may come from different places in the world, may be hesitant about sharing information about themselves through administrative data, which they are not confident about being kept safe, secure. They don't know whether it'll be confidential.

They may be a younger person living in a conservative family who is uncomfortable about their parents seeing them for who they are. This is so for the whole of the rainbow communities, LGBTIQ+, and all the MVP, FAF, Pasifika terms. These are issues of social stigma. And the 2023 census, as you will know, was the first time that people were able to self-identify and disclose confidentially information about themselves in a way that meant that they could be counted, and that that information could then feed into planning for

services, for a whole range of areas where it's important to be counted, like 173,000 people on the LGBT+ communities in Aotearoa in 2023. And Statistics NZ admits that's an underestimate because there are still concerns about that confidentiality and trust.

But if we compare that to administrative data first, and you asked agencies to give a count, you would probably get maybe 10% of that. As a bisexual person, I have never been asked my sexual orientation by any government agency that has recorded my information before that 2023 census.

So that is invisible information about me. The same with people from different iwi and hapū who may never have been when they're-- They might identify as Māori, but it may not go any further. They may not be asked that. So administrative data has got massive gaps, and that is a risk. So as you will have seen from my...

I echo a lot of previous submissions about even if we were living in a perfect society where people felt confident sharing information about themselves, we're not there yet, and yet that information is really important. And there's lots of things I could say, but I'll-- Oh, and just that when I say it's really important, I used to help run hackathons using sort of data that's been collected from sources, and I know how important it is to business, but I'll stop there. Any questions?

Right. Thank you, Kaye. I think- Question. That's very good. Points well made. And thank you. As we are busy, you're also busy in that- Yes... you've got a lengthy submission, so thank you for that today. Yes. And I'll just show this to the point of the camera that I don't know if it can be seen, but I'm wearing badges so that when I'm traveling, people can know that I'm safe to engage with and I'm being counted. But as I say, lots of people don't have that luxury.

Okay. Thank you very much. Right. Now, Elizabeth Walker, I think, is that right? Hello. Hello. Hey, Elizabeth. We can hear you. Great. We got five minutes with you. Thank you. My name is Elizabeth Walker. Good afternoon. I'm here today to ask the committee to commission an independent report expert review of the admin data first model and consequent cessation of the periodic census of populations and dwellings before it's too late.

Just put my camera on. I have- Oh, hello. You heard my intro? Yes. Okay. I have over 40 years' experience working in the data modeling, management, and governance of data. I worked for New Zealand Statistics Department, DataBank New Zealand, Westpac New Zealand, and then had 20 years with Teradata Data Warehouse, where for 10 years I governed all Asia Pacific client data projects across industries and government.

I've encountered a lot of data designs and data problems, especially data gaps. When clients had manual or perhaps no source systems in place for crucial data items, data warehouse projects were inevitably delayed or halted, and the client's ensuing business plans and projects were also put on hold.

I'm humbled by the range and depth of expert testimony here today. Please consider the impact of data gaps and low-quality data on New Zealand Government's decision-making and change management capabilities, as so many people described. I believe that data and data quality are the lifeblood of every organization and every government in our modern digital world.

And without the census data, you've heard that we stand to lose the kete, or basket, or spine perhaps that connects, and contains all our people information, identifying individuals, dwelling location, ages, relationships to their households, and in most cases, that's their family.We'll lose granular data about individuals and families coming directly from people. That's you and me, so it's more accurate and probably the most highly trusted data in government and beyond. It's more than a century of continuous high-quality

longitudinal data, and data that directly affects electoral boundaries, foundation of our democracy. Apart from the census, no government department counts and locates all people present in New Zealand, as you've heard. And I don't think I can be represented in the same way as a taxpayer, a beneficiary, a patient, a land holder. Government departments hold only slim slices of data, as we would say.

New Zealand's population statistics are key pieces of our national infrastructure. I view the census data as playing a pivotal role in New Zealand's national population statistics. I was pleased to see Koi Tū come up before me. I was going to talk about how much I supported their proposals and how integral I saw the census as part of the population strategy that they were talking about.

I can say more, but to conclude, along with the repercussions that experts here describe, it's the lack of process and rigor that I've heard this morning in reaching this point that is perhaps most disturbing. Please reconsider our loss of population and family data, and commission an independent expert review of the decisions and options regarding the census before it is abandoned. Thank you very much for listening.

Are there any questions? Yeah. Thank you, Elizabeth. Now, any questions from anyone? This is good. Straightforward. No? Thank you, Elizabeth. That's very good. And we've got a number of submissions on the same topic, so I think we'll get a pretty comprehensive view on the issues. But thank you very much for your contribution.
Thank you. Right. Now, we've got Steve Farrow, is it? Steve, please come to the table. We've got five minutes with you.

Steve. Hi, I'm Steve Farrow. I thank the Select Committee and its administrators for providing me with the opportunity to make this oral submission. I assume you have read my written submission- We could... or have it in front of you, so I will not challenge your intellect by reading it to you.

My background, I studied history and political science and other stuff at University of Canterbury in the first half of the 1970s. Then I spent my working life as a high school teacher, and then a union membership educator. The only difference between those two roles was that union members didn't view me as a boss, so didn't tell me to F off.

I'm now happily retired, but still interested in New Zealand current affairs and political and equity issues. One thing the committee needs to know is that I take this opportunity seriously. That is why the Clerk of the House will receive a bill from me for the trees I have used and the ink cartridges I have emptied to print and read the document for an omnibus bill, and that's really hard, especially for individual submitters.

My written submission canvassed the high-level concerns I have. The bill and the documents that support it, as have been mentioned just before us, have sort of allayed my concerns about the effects on the Electoral Act and process to adjust constituency boundaries.

I think one document told me that entrenched clauses of the act would remain. That I know about them proves that I listened during my pol sci lectures way back when. I'm convinced that Admin Data First should be able to provide the data required.

What really concerns me is that, as we have heard already, Admin Data First will not have the data required to know about really important stuff. Government health data does not capture and is often withheld from the government by minority, but important segments of the population, Maori, Pasifika, disabled, women, gender diverse, youth, GLBTQI++++, homeless, poorly housed, unemployed, those suffering mental

health issues, those recently released from prison, et cetera. Even the proposed census attribute surveys, I think they're called, will not capture such groups unless well-constructed and probably larger than the 3% to 5% of the population that has been posited. A friend of mine even more retired than me spent his working life as a senior statistician.He had never heard of the admin data first methodology,

and the survey sample sizes are very concerning to him. An equally worrying feature is the movement of the 2028 census to 2030. The only reason given is to allow the government statistician and government entities to get their ducks in a row.

Over the last 150 odd years, the only missed censuses were for issues of biblical proportions: world depression, world war, earthquake catastrophe, and world pestilence. Do such as civil service convenience and seriously bad weather now count as of biblical importance?

I do not want to argue with others who I'm sure have argued for themselves. But as an individual, I'm very concerned, uncomfortable, about the intention to move the 2028 census to 2030, for the effect it will have on Maori in particular. That move explicitly shifts consultation with Maori to the new date rather than before the 2029 election.

Why can't the new super and modern system not begin such consultation from about now? You may think I'm a grumpy old man. I see on the oral submitters list that you will soon hear from Brian Easton. He wouldn't know me from a bar of soap, but I remember him as a relatively young economics lecturer who frequented the town site student cafeteria in Christchurch in the early 1970s. You'll find out whether the Data and Statistics Census Amendment Bill makes him grumpier than me.

Thank you for your time, and I'm happy for questions should there be any. Hey, thanks, Steve. Thoughtful. Hey. Thank you. Very thoughtful submission. Right. Mr. Craig Atkinson, is it? Craig, are you with us?

Craig, are you with us? All right, cool. Hello. Hello. We can hear you. We can see you. Hey, Craig. We've seen you before. Kia ora. We've seen you before. Good to have you with us again. Good to be with the GSC, the GSC, and that again. Yeah, no, I'd keep it very short for you.

I don't agree with it. I think that you've, by the professional people that specialize in this area, pointed out, it's been tried in 20 countries, and not worked, and you really haven't got a clue. And you're just guessing at what to do. I think there's a point with algorithms that comes up with election times and all that information that you collect on us.

And yeah, so no, I disagree with it. Put this one in the bin with the rest of them. Thank you. All right. Hey, thank you. Any- Straightforward. Last time we called, it was a beautiful day, and it looks like another beautiful day. It's always sunshine in Paihia, mate. I know. That's what you always say.
Hey, thank you very much. Kia ora. Thank you. Bye-bye. Right. Now, We Create, Mr. Paul Browning, is it? Paul, are you with us?

Yes. Good afternoon. Hello. Nice to hear from you. Oh, sorry, it's Paula, is it? This is Paula, yes. Oh, sorry, I had you down as Paul. So, apologies for that. Nice to have you with us this afternoon, Paula. Mm. Kia ora, and thank you for having me.

I'm the executive director of We Create. We Create is the alliance of New Zealand's creative industry associations and creative organizations, representing over 30,000 Kiwi creatives and creative businesses across all creative industries, from games, to music, to screen, to publishing, fashion, architecture, advertising, design, performing, and visual arts.

We note that the creative sector is highly digital in how the products and services we make and sell are created, marketed, distributed, and consumed. We're here today because this bill matters, not just to the government and statisticians, but to every creative professional in this country whose work and value is currently mostly invisible to government data systems.

We Create has engaged with the New Zealand government data system for over a decade. We commissioned NZIR to produce the first comprehensive valuation of the creative sector in 2016 because government data could not tell that story. In 2026, it still cannot tell it accurately. As it currently operates, the data system does not see us, and without deliberate intervention, this bill will lock in and magnify that invisibility.

The creative industries are not a niche concern for the data system. The Ministry for Culture and Heritage reports that the sector contributed 18.5 billion to GDP in 2025 and employed 122,000 New Zealanders or 4.4% of the total workforce.Creative sectors are defined by UNCTAD and many advanced economies as high-value economic contributors.

In New Zealand, the Cabinet-approved Amplify Creative and Cultural Strategy sets out an evidence framework as a core government commitment and deliverable under the strategy. That commitment cannot be delivered if the underlying data infrastructure is broken. The data system used by Infometrics to produce data for MCH continues to draw from data systems that do not accurately reflect the creative sector and occupations as they exist today, and have no mechanism for keeping up with technology change.

If we transition to an admin data-first census model without fixing the foundations of the existing system, we will entrench what is already broken. The bill's regulatory impact statement acknowledges that certain communities will be harder to measure under the new model and may require tailored solutions. The creative sector meets this definition in every respect. Let me be specific about why.

The freelance and portfolio work reality. The creative sector has double the number of freelancers and contractors of any other sector in the economy. Our workforce is characterized by multi-occupation, project-based, and good structured employment. Admin data systems built around the employer-employee model are structurally ill-equipped to capture this reality.

Most of government policy, including in education and vocational training, is still predicated on an employment model that is simply outdated for our sector. That must change, and this bill is the place to begin fixing it. The IRD single occupation model currently allows only a single occupation to be reported.

For a sound engineer who also teaches, composes, and earns royalties from a streaming platform, that single field is not just incomplete, it is misleading. If IRD data becomes a pillar of the new census model, these gaps become structural features, not temporary limitations. The classification code problem is still unresolved.

ANZSIC and ANZSOC codes must be updated to reflect the realities of modern and evolving creative and digital economy. The census transition should not proceed without addressing these gaps. The digital industries and the rear view mirror problem. Every industry in our membership is digital, yet existing data systems do not capture the breadth or depth of value created by digital creative work. The new census model must build in live mechanisms to capture emerging digital industries and occupations on an ongoing basis, not simply take a

snapshot of where the economy was at the last classification review. The investment and infrastructure gap. We want to be direct with the committee about something that underlies all of this. The minister's announcement framed this bill primarily around cost reduction. Where Create's concern is that building a new census model on admin data that already misrepresents and underrepresents the creative sector and doing so to save money is a false economy.

We are skeptical that data of sufficient quality and accuracy sits in government agencies, and that it can be extracted into the data system using resources within agencies' existing budget lines. Data is an economic asset. Its value is seen most clearly in big tech and artificial intelligence companies, where vast volumes of data underpin revenue at scale.

The New Zealand government has not sufficiently prioritized or invested in data systems, particularly in the last three decades, as technology change made better data capture more feasible, not less. We have seen what a deliberate and resourced approach to data can deliver. When Toi Māi, the Workforce Development Council for Creative, Cultural, Tech and Recreation sectors was established, it invested in a dedicated data and insights team.

That team embedded data thinking at the very start of every project. In many cases, data and insights were captured that would otherwise have not existed and were subsequently useful well beyond their original purpose. That is the level of intentionality and investment that is needed across government if the outcomes that this bill sets out are to be achieved.

Here is what we are asking the committee to do. That Stats NZ formally designate the creative sector as a harder to measure community, requiring tailored data collection solutions, and that this designation be written into the transition planning, not left to discretion. Secondly, that the outstanding classification issues with ANZSIC and ANZSOC codes be resolved before the 2030 census transition.

Thirdly, that We Create's Data and Insights working group be formally recognized as a co-design partner in the design of the Census Attribute Survey. Fourthly, that the single occupation limitation on IRD admin data be addressed as part of the cross-government admin data improvement program. And finally, that Stats NZ be required to track and publicly report on creative sector data quality as part of the periodic review obligations under the amended act.

In closing, the creative sector is one of Aotearoa's most dynamic economic contributors and one of its least visible in official data. We've been making this point to government for over a decade. This bill is an opportunity to finally close that gap or to lock it in for another generation. We are asking you to choose the former.

We're grateful for the opportunity to present to you today, and I'd welcome any questions you might have. Thank you, Paula, and particularly recognizing your sector. It is huge and growing, and hopefully grows even more rapidly. So thank you for a bit of a passionate plea about that. Any questions? Got one from Camilla Village.

Thank you. We've heard from some other submitters who have concerns about the data, similar to what you've raised in your submission, only for different sectors. And I've just been wanting to follow through with them on what the impact of not improving the data quality would be. So my question for you really is, if these changes aren't made, what is the impact likely to be for your sector?

It's stagnation in government policy development. I can give you an example. We're still not counting digital exports in this country, so the value of what the creative sector is exporting isn't visible in government data. When you're looking at what shapes our industry, we're still, another example, if you're a game developer, you're a software engineer. So we're not understanding where the work is happening, where the workforces are, or valuable things like what's happening with exports. That leads to policy developments that

we're constantly having to go, "What about us?" in. And being present in places where really the data should be telling the story about where government policy and support should be heading. As industry, we don't feel that we should have to invest quite so much time in doing that to help government do its job.

All right, good. I've got a question from Carl Bates. Thanks very much, Chair. Thanks for the presentation, Paula, really appreciated it. You make a very strong argument around the data for your sector. My question is, do you feel that that will be more or less possible in the context of the traditional census? Or do you think that an admin-first approach with the additional focused data collection for industries like yours could achieve that outcome?

I'm definitely in the and, and camp, not the either or. I think there is way more we need to do with securing good data in this country that lets us make informed decisions. I'm not suggesting that that solely sits with government, but government obviously has a key role in holding core data about what's happening in the country and the economy as it relates to policy development.

We have our own industry who correlate their own data. You'll know from what the committee's been looking at that getting data doesn't come cheaply. So we've got industries with small budgets trying to create their own data in the absence of government data. What we need is that government has the core data, and that anything the industry is doing is over and above that in an area that's of particular interest to the industry.

The government having a solid base of core data, I think is the important thing. All right. Hey, thank you, Paula, for your contribution this afternoon. Very interesting. Thank you for having me. Right. Now, we're going to Greater Wellington. I see that we've got a few people on the line. And please come to-- Are you on your own here?

But we've got friends- I am... as well. We've got some on Zoom, have we? Yeah. You should have Darren. Oh, she's something else. Okay. Are you on your own or- Yes. Do you want to come up, Fiona, just in case? Yes, please. Mm-hmm. Fiona's one of our officers. Yeah, good. Fantastic. And we have Darren Potra chairing online. Yeah.

Online? Yes. Are they online? Not yet. But anyway, let's not hesitate. If they come online, we'll happily join them. All right. Thank you. Can I just ask a question whether the staff have got this presentation? If not, I can just talk to it anyway. Say that again. Have the staff got a... Oh, no, he was going to put it online.

That's okay. We have copies. You have copies. Oh, yeah. Do you want to hand them around? Yeah. It's always a really good idea to have copies. All right, fine. Thank you. But we have got your submission as well, so. That's brilliant. Yeah. Thank you. And- Fine. So just take it as read. I'll take it as read. And we're all fast readers here. Thank you. We can share even better. No. Because we're a collegial group up here.

Excellent. Great to hear. All right, fantastic. Let's proceed. All right. I'll introduce myself first. I'm Claire Johnston. I'm a regional councilor, first term, representing the Porirua, Tawa area, but this presentation is, of course, on the whole region. Why are we presenting on this bill?

We're doing this because as an organization making evidence-based investment decisions for our communities means that we have to actually understand our data to make those decisions. It's pivotable. Robust data matters. We're responsible as a regional council for long-term network and place-based planning across transport, land use, infrastructure, emergency and hazard management, and of course, for resilience.

We plan and invest over a 20 to 50 year timeframe, and often for assets that will span across many generations. Our decisions balance the growth, safety, equity, and environmental protection, and should always be value for money. We're required by legislation to base our decisions on sound evidence and robust data. As a councilor, I always expect evidence and robust data.

Regional spatial planning and how we plan our region and how we consolidate urban growth around existing growth corridors, how we pair urban growth with economic development, how we stage growth over time, all matter. The bulk water supply is another issue. How do we anticipate population growth with water supply when we make these investment decisions?

Water, of course, is up for debate at the moment, but it's really important you have consistent information and understand where your region's going. Rural and urban flood protection, the level we build at flood protection and schemes, is that one to 50 protection? Is it one to 500 protection? Or do we take into account, since January, I think we've had 72 incidents of national or district emergency management issues.

Public transport is big for the Greater Wellington Regional Council. It's probably the area that I get asked the most questions about-We have to have really good information, a really good understanding of our population and where our population works, and where our population goes to school, and where they get to.

So why does it matter to us? Why census? GWRC combines the latest science with historical data, current information, future forecasting. We also have to understand the patterns. We use these assessments to develop our policy to make the investment decisions, land use planning, and as I said, emergency planning.

We can't function on fragmented population data. Disaggregated and administrative data sets only work when anchored to a strong, centrally collected national population count. I've personally watched, over the years, data that isn't collected properly or it's inconsistent in its collection. I've seen it particularly in economic data.

If you weaken the national collection, it leaves regional councils carrying planning, emergency, fiscal, and equity consequences without a reliable evidence base. Census data presents a single source of truth that we and our communities and iwi can build and get behind when we're making decisions.

Central population statistics are critical to regional infrastructure. They provide a single authoritative population baseline that we rely on. Decisions to invest in 30 to 100-year lifespans must be based on good data. Emergency management depends on that data.

Hazard management depends on it. So does statutory planning, and legal defensibility is weakened if you don't have evidence. The costs and the responsibility could fall onto councils if we have no solution. That concerns me because collecting data is imperative. You can't have inconsistency.

You can't have one region collecting data one way and one collecting it another. There must be consistency. It also affects you as policymakers. If you can't see the whole, you risk fragmentation in your own policy decisions. So the bottom line for us is weakening national population collection does not empower regions.

It fragments the system, it increases the risk, and it undermines some of those critical infrastructural decisions. We do support innovation, and the use of administrative data needs to improve. We acknowledge there have been problems with efficiency and timeliness.

However, it's essential that the new model continues to provide accurate, trusted, and sufficiently granular population data at a regional, territorial, and community level, as well as your national level. To conclude, it comes down to the quality and detail of the data. We're reminded that New Zealand does not have a great track record in large-scale data projects.

The challenge to this select committee is to ensure that for the transition, organizations like Greater Wellington will continue to have robust and reliable data from which to make our decisions. Flying blind in the future does not really bear thinking about. We cannot do that.

Right. Mr. Ponter, do you want to say anything, or anyone else? You're on mute, Darren. Kia ora. Kia ora, Mr. Chair. Kia ora, members. No, very eloquently presented by Councillor Johnson. Thank you. I think the underlying thing here is that we are making billion-dollar decisions. We're often doing that on a co-investment basis with the government.

And so the issues that Claire has put on the table with you about reliable data relates as much as anything else to our relationship with our co-funding partner, which in most cases is the government. Okay, cool. Right. Members, questions? Right. Camilla Village.

Thank you. Thanks both of you here, and Darren as well, for your attendance and for your submission. We did have a question in an earlier submission about the use of data collected through rates, and I just wanted to put it to you as the first council that we've spoken to since then. What information do you get from collecting rates, and what's the limitation on that in terms of the overall information that you can gain through- I can make a comment on where I think the limitations are, but I'm going to let Diana or Darren to speak to it.

Yeah. So, thank you, Camilla. A rather unique situation for regional councils. Most regional councils rate via the territorial authority. So, you receive your... Well, if you're in Auckland, it's a different set of circumstances altogether. But in this region, the Kāpiti District Council, for example, rates for us in the Kāpiti area. They get that data. We don't generally interrogate that data.

We wouldn't generally base large investment decisions like a billion-dollar water collection scheme, which is on the cards for us, we wouldn't generally base that on rating information. We would base that on census projections about how the population is likely to change over the next 20 to 50 years.

All right. Hey, well, thank you for your submission. Thank you for coming in person. That's always nice. But comprehensive submission. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you. And following on, and with that same vein, we're going to Local Government New Zealand
They're on the line, you can see them? Yeah. Okay. If you're there, we're looking forward to hearing from you, from local government.

Kia ora. Hello, Fran. How are you going? I'm well, thank you. Good to see you today. So I'm here as Mayor of South Wairarapa, with Palmerston North Mayor, Grant Smith, who I hope is also online somewhere. Hey, so Fran, sorry to interrupt, we can't see you. It's very, very blurry. So- You can't s- how's that?

No, we can't see anything. We just see a blurry- Okay... something in front of your camera, I think. My video is on. That's weird. I can see you p- oh, you're right. It is blurry. It's like something's- I can see it... in front of the camera. You're just about in- No, my camera is completely free. Um- Anyway... and I don't, I can't... I'm not sure what's happened there.

Doesn't matter. We can hear you, for sure. I don't want to get out of it and get in again because I won't get in again. No. So if it's okay, maybe I'll just talk. I'm not sure- Yes. Yeah, that's great... why the camera is obscured, but I'm looking, doing it on my laptop and there's nothing in front of it. So, I'm here for Local Government New Zealand, and Grant Smith also. So councils are now seeing their populations changing in more diverse ways than we ever have in the last 50 years, and to plan ahead, we need population stats about small

areas. For example, measures such as the Deprivation Index, which is derived from the census and provides mesh block data. Every council is going to be affected differently by the growth in the elderly population, but there are other trends impacting also. For example, changes in farming practice, less dry stock, more forestry, have had major population impacts in some rural areas. And on the other hand, in urban areas, growth in retirement villages or growing concentrations of townhouses have driven changes in demand for services.

Although population projections provide reliable signals of things to come, councils also need much more local knowledge. Currently, there's just insufficient evidence that the use of the records from government agencies, which will be dated, will give us the data we need. Councils are in the front line in the provision of information and managing public interest, but in an increasingly cost-con- constrained environment with a rates cap looming.

We see this every day. For example, we're managing historical underinvestment in infrastructure. The cost of relocating this capital investment would be prohibitive once it's established. So we need to get as much data as possible, along the lines of what Greater Wellington have just said. Regarding services, there's low political tolerance of loss or reduction resulting from change in population structure, and we don't want to be in a worse position than we are now in forecasting future needs.

We're currently in a period of radical population change, and after two failed censuses, local government needs assurance that there's more behind this than third time lucky. There've been increasing calls for the select committee to establish an independent review of the proposals, and we support that. Lastly, it's ironic that we're here today by accident rather than being properly involved by Stats New Zealand.

As a significantly affected group, we'd expect to have had a high level of engagement on such sweeping changes. Local government should be seen by Stats NZ as a useful partner with which they can work, even when planning a census. We can provide local knowledge to enable robust decisions about the resources required to do the job properly.

This didn't happen in 2018 or '23. There were preventable mistakes, such as Haast being missed out completely and Kawerau grossly undercounted. Saving money by abandoning the census or not undertaking it properly is just wrong, and future planning will be seriously impacted by not having adequate, correct data on which to base decisions.

Thank you, Andrew, Mr Chair. That's all for me, and- Okay... hopefully Grant will have a couple of comments, and then we've got questions if you wish. Grant, please. Yes. Hi, Chair Andrew. Thanks, Mayor Fran. Look, I think Fran has summed up all the detail very well. But look, we understand the fiscal conditions are tight.

But a census is really, really fundamental to effective decision-making. We're just going through our annual budget, and giving an example, our Pasifika community, which is quite large with about sort of 6 to 7,000 people, came in and they do feel some of the data around their sector isn't as good as it could be. So, abandoning a proper census would possibly make that even worse.

Look, we do think most Western countries have a form of a census. It isn't the traditional one survey type, but there are many different hybrids. And we do think that if we don't do that, and the 2004 independent evaluation panel said New Zealand wasn't ready to move to the administration model.

So we do, from a position of knowing our local communities really, really well, we're absolutely sensing this could be an absolute disaster for future planning. If there isn't a little bit of thought given to, and again, I understand the fiscal requirements, but putting back something in that census space, because we've just seen how unreliable some of the individual government departments and administrative budgeting has been.
